ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic kebab food product class; no specific Hara Bhara Kebab entry exists. Closest defensible parent class used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated Hara Bhara Kebab code. A0CKP (vegetable-based patties and fritters) is the closest defensible category given the spinach-pea-potato composition and shallow-frying preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Hara Bhara Kebab or sufficiently close vegetarian kebab product.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard appetizer serving of Hara Bhara Kebab consists of 4–5 pieces weighing approximately 150 g, commonly served as a starter or snack in Indian restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore Food Composition Database — Hara Bhara Kebab
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values for energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, and cholesterol are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (confidence 0.95). Saturated fat (3.33 g), trans fat (0.07 g), and vitamin C (13.33 mg) are from a secondary source with lower confidence (0.70) and are flagged accordingly in notes. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and remaining micronutrients not reported by HPB are set to -1.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index values for boiled potato and green peas — analogous estimate for Hara Bhara Kebab
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Hara Bhara Kebab exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the dominant carbohydrate contributors: boiled potato (GI approximately 65–70, University of Sydney GI Database) and green peas (GI approximately 48, University of Sydney GI Database). The shallow-frying process and high fat content (26.4 g/100 g) slow gastric emptying, moderating the effective GI toward the lower end of the medium range. Glycemic load per 150 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (65 × 7.86) / 100 ≈ 5.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1080 mg per 100 g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100 g upper threshold for hypertension-safe foods
hypertension.guidance: Hara Bhara Kebab is strongly unsuitable for individuals with hypertension due to sodium at 1080 mg/100 g, primarily from chaat masala and added salt; patients should avoid this dish or request a significantly reduced-salt preparation and limit portion to no more than 50 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate GI (~65) driven by potato content; low net carbohydrate (5.24 g/100 g) partially offset by high fat (26.4 g/100 g) from shallow-frying
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low carbohydrate content (5.24 g/100 g) and moderate protein (10.7 g/100 g) support blood glucose stability, but the high fat from shallow-frying slows digestion and may cause delayed postprandial glucose rise; individuals with type 2 diabetes should prefer a baked version and limit portion to 100–120 g.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (26.4 g/100 g), elevated sodium (1080 mg/100 g), and cholesterol (85 mg/100 g) present compounded cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of high total fat, elevated sodium, and cholesterol makes this dish unsuitable for regular consumption by individuals with cardiovascular disease; a baked preparation with reduced salt and no paneer would substantially lower the cardiovascular risk profile.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Shallow-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that worsen joint inflammation; sodium at 1080 mg/100 g exacerbates tissue swelling; potato is a nightshade vegetable
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid the shallow-fried preparation due to AGE formation and high sodium; a baked version with reduced chaat masala and salt, and substitution of potato with a non-nightshade starch, is recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hara Bhara Kebab (Hindi: हरा भरा कबाब, meaning 'green and lush kebab') is a popular Indian vegetarian appetizer made from a spiced mixture of spinach (palak), green peas (matar), boiled potato, and paneer (Indian cottage cheese), bound with breadcrumbs and seasoned with mint, fresh coriander, green chilli, ginger, and chaat masala. The mixture is shaped into flat round patties and shallow-fried in oil until golden and crisp on the outside while remaining soft and moist inside. The dish originates from North Indian cuisine and is widely served in Indian restaurants across Singapore and the broader South Asian diaspora. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 302 kcal, 10.7 g protein, 26.4 g fat (3.33 g saturated), 5.24 g carbohydrate, 2.85 g sugar, and 1080 mg sodium. The high sodium content (1080 mg/100 g) makes it unsuitable for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. The moderate glycemic index (~65) and low net carbohydrate content make it conditionally acceptable for type 2 diabetes when portion-controlled and prepared by baking rather than frying. Spinach contributes iron (1.15 mg/100 g), calcium (118 mg/100 g), vitamin C (13.33 mg/100 g), and potassium (387 mg/100 g). The dish is halal and vegetarian but contains dairy (paneer) and gluten (breadcrumbs). It is commonly served as a starter or snack at lunch and dinner, typically in portions of 4–5 pieces (~150 g).

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients and primary micronutrients sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C are from a secondary source (confidence 0.70) and are flagged in notes. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database based on the dominant carbohydrate contributors (potato and green peas), with full documentation of the estimation basis.
notes: 1. dietary_fibre_g is reported as 0.0 by HPB Singapore Food Composition Database. This is a confirmed reported value from the approved source, not an assumption; however, it is nutritionally unexpected for a spinach-pea-potato mixture and may reflect the specific preparation tested (e.g., heavily processed or strained). Retained as reported. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile and full micronutrient panel for this specific Hara Bhara Kebab preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database or ICMR-NIN as of 2026. 3. saturated_fat_g (3.33), trans_fat_g (0.07), and vitamin_c_mg (13.33) are sourced from a secondary source with confidence 0.70; these values are plausible but should be verified against a primary approved source in a future review cycle. These secondary values are not from a delivery platform or forbidden source but from a supplementary nutritional panel associated with the input record. 4. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on boiled potato (GI ~65–70) and green peas (GI ~48) from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Hara Bhara Kebab was found in any approved source. 5. Food subgroup updated from 'Other meats' to 'Vegetarian meat alternatives' to accurately reflect the plant-based composition. 6. Chinese local names (绿色蔬菜烤饼 / 綠色蔬菜烤餅) are descriptive translations; no established Chinese culinary name exists for this dish in Singapore usage.
